preamble.summary: Addition A, placed before Esther 1 in the LXX, provides a theological overture to the entire book. Mordecai receives a prophetic dream featuring two great dragons prepared for battle, a cosmic conflict threatening the righteous nation, and a tiny spring that becomes a great river of salvation. He also discovers a conspiracy against the king, establishing his loyalty. The dream will be interpreted in Addition F at the book's end.
preamble.remarkable: The dream transforms the book of Esther from a secular court narrative into a story of divine providence operating through history. The tiny-spring-to-great-river image is one of the most beautiful metaphors in Jewish literature for God's use of the seemingly insignificant to accomplish cosmic purposes. The addition also solves a narrative problem: in the MT, Mordecai's discovery of the conspiracy (2:21-23) seems abrupt; here it receives a proper prologue.
preamble.friction: This addition has no Hebrew counterpart and is absent from the MT. Its apocalyptic imagery (dragons, cosmic warfare) is foreign to the MT Esther's this-worldly narrative style. The dream genre connects Esther to the Daniel tradition but may sit uneasily with the book's original literary character.
preamble.connections: Esther 2:21-23 (conspiracy parallel); Daniel 2, 7 (dream-vision genre); Joel 2:28-32 (prophetic dreams); Addition F (dream interpretation); Revelation 12 (dragon imagery in cosmic conflict).

verses.text_source: Greek: Ἔτους δευτέρου βασιλεύοντος Ἀρταξέρξου τοῦ μεγάλου
verses.text_reference: In the second year of the reign of Artaxerxes the Great, on the first day of Nisan, Mordecai son of Jair son of Shimei son of Kish, of the tribe of Benjamin, had a dream.
verses.rendering: In the second year of the reign of Artaxerxes the Great, on the first day of Nisan, Mordecai son of Jair, son of Shimei, son of Kish, of the tribe of Benjamin, had a dream.
translator_notes: Addition A opens the entire book in the LXX, placing Mordecai's prophetic dream before the banquet of chapter 1. This provides a theological framework for the 'coincidences' that follow.

verses.text_source: Greek: ἄνθρωπος Ιουδαῖος οἰκῶν ἐν Σούσοις τῇ πόλει
verses.text_reference: He was a Jew living in the city of Susa, a great man, serving in the court of the king.
verses.rendering: He was a Jew dwelling in the city of Susa, a prominent man who served in the court of the king.

verses.text_source: Greek: ἦν δὲ ἐκ τῆς αἰχμαλωσίας ἧς ᾐχμαλώτευσεν Ναβουχοδονοσορ
verses.text_reference: He was one of the captives whom King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon had brought from Jerusalem with King Jeconiah of Judea.
verses.rendering: He was one of the captives whom King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon had carried away from Jerusalem with King Jeconiah of Judah.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ τοῦτο αὐτοῦ τὸ ἐνύπνιον
verses.text_reference: And this was his dream: there were noises and confusion, thunders and earthquake, tumult on the earth.
verses.rendering: And this was his dream: there were cries and noise, thunder and earthquake, and turmoil upon the earth.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἰδοὺ δύο δράκοντες μεγάλοι ἕτοιμοι προῆλθον
verses.text_reference: Then two great dragons came forward, both ready to fight, and they roared terribly.
verses.rendering: And look — two great dragons came forward, both prepared for battle, and they roared mightily.
translator_notes: The two dragons represent Mordecai and Haman (as revealed in Addition F). The apocalyptic imagery — earthquakes, dragons, cosmic conflict — transforms Esther from a court tale into a story of cosmic significance.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἐγένετο ἀπὸ τῆς φωνῆς αὐτῶν πᾶν ἔθνος ἡτοιμάσθη εἰς πόλεμον
verses.text_reference: At their roaring every nation prepared for war, to fight against the righteous nation.
verses.rendering: At the sound of their roaring, every nation prepared for war, to fight against the nation of the righteous.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἡμέρα σκότους καὶ γνόφου
verses.text_reference: It was a day of darkness and gloom, of tribulation and distress, affliction and great tumult on the earth.
verses.rendering: And look — a day of darkness and gloom, of tribulation and distress, affliction and great turmoil upon the earth.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἐταράχθη πᾶν ἔθνος δίκαιον
verses.text_reference: And the whole righteous nation was troubled; they feared the evils that threatened them.
verses.rendering: And the whole righteous nation was troubled, fearing the evils that threatened them,
and they prepared themselves to perish.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἐβόησαν πρὸς τὸν θεόν
verses.text_reference: Then they cried out to God; and at their outcry, as though from a tiny spring, there came a great river, with abundant water.
verses.rendering: Then they cried out to God. And from their cry, as though from a tiny spring, there came a great river with abundant water.
translator_notes: The tiny spring becoming a great river symbolizes Esther — small and insignificant, yet through her the salvation of the entire people flows. This is revealed in Addition F.

verses.text_source: Greek: φῶς ἐγένετο καὶ ὁ ἥλιος ἀνέτειλεν
verses.text_reference: Light came, and the sun rose, and the lowly were exalted and devoured those held in honor.
verses.rendering: Light dawned and the sun rose, and the lowly were exalted and consumed those held in honor.

verses.text_source: Greek: Μαρδοχαῖος ὁ ἰδὼν τὸ ἐνύπνιον τοῦτο
verses.text_reference: Mordecai saw in this dream what God had determined to do, and after he awoke he bore it in mind.
verses.rendering: Mordecai, who had seen this dream and what God had determined to do, kept it in his heart after he awoke,

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἡσύχασεν ἕως νυκτὸς βουλόμενος γνῶναι
verses.text_reference: and until night he tried in every way to understand it.
verses.rendering: and he sought in every way to understand it until nightfall.

verses.text_source: Greek: Καὶ ἦν Μαρδοχαῖος ἀναπαυόμενος ἐν τῇ αὐλῇ
verses.text_reference: Now Mordecai was resting in the courtyard with Gabatha and Tharra, two eunuchs of the king who kept watch in the courtyard.
verses.rendering: Now Mordecai was resting in the courtyard with Gabatha and Tharra, two eunuchs of the king who guarded the courtyard.

verses.text_source: Greek: ἤκουσέν τε αὐτῶν τοὺς λογισμούς
verses.text_reference: He overheard their conversation and inquired into their purposes, and learned that they were preparing to lay hands on King Artaxerxes; and he informed the king concerning them.
verses.rendering: He overheard their plotting and investigated their designs, and discovered that they were preparing to assassinate King Artaxerxes. He reported this to the king.
translator_notes: This conspiracy plot parallels Esther 2:21-23 in the MT. The LXX provides it here as a prologue, establishing Mordecai's loyalty before the main narrative begins.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἐξήτασεν ὁ βασιλεὺς τοὺς δύο εὐνούχους
verses.text_reference: Then the king examined the two eunuchs, and after they confessed they were led to execution.
verses.rendering: Then the king examined the two eunuchs, and when they confessed they were led away to execution.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἔγραψεν ὁ βασιλεὺς τοὺς λόγους τούτους εἰς μνημόσυνον
verses.text_reference: The king made a record of these things, and Mordecai also made a record of them. The king ordered Mordecai to serve in the court and rewarded him for these things. But Haman son of Hammedatha, a Bougean, was in great honor with the king, and he sought to injure Mordecai and his people because of the two eunuchs of the king.
verses.rendering: The king recorded these events as a memorial, and Mordecai also recorded them. Then the king ordered Mordecai to serve in the court and rewarded him for this. But Haman, son of Hammedatha, a Bougaean, who was held in great honor by the king, sought to harm Mordecai and his people on account of the two eunuchs of the king.
translator_notes: The LXX calls Haman a 'Bougaean' (Bougaios) rather than an 'Agagite' as in the MT. This may be a Greek corruption of 'Agagite' or a deliberate change to avoid the Amalekite connection. Some scholars read it as 'braggart.' The verse establishes Haman's enmity before the main narrative begins.

translator_notes.note: Greek Bougaios — the LXX's designation for Haman, replacing the MT's 'Agagite.' The meaning is debated; it may be a transliteration error or deliberate substitution.
